headline: James Wood Just Broke the League — And Nobody Was Ready For It
subheadline: A .636 OBP, 40 runs scored, and a history-obliterating week from Athletic Park has the rest of BeerLeague Baseball doing the math on how scared they should be.
body:
**James Wood posted a .636 OBP for the Athletic Park Dragons this week — the highest single-week OBP mark in league history, shattering the previous record of .414 set by General Hospital in Week 1. For context: Willson Contreras also hit .594 OBP this week, which would have been the record in any other week. On any other team. In any other timeline.**

## At-a-Glance

| Matchup | Score | Player of the Matchup |
|---------|-------|-----------------------|
| Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs def. Albuquerque Bombers | 5–4 | Wonderdogs' pitching staff (62 Ks, 1.13 WHIP, 7 NSVH) |
| Sugar Land Skeeters def. Abbotsford-Colby Twins | 8–2 | Sugar Land pitching staff (2.11 ERA, 8 NSVH) |
| Athletic Park Dragons def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers | 6–3 | James Wood (.636 OBP, historic week) |
| BaseballPro77 def. North Correa | 5–3 | Oneil Cruz (11 SB for North Correa, though in a losing effort) |
| Riverside Roughnecks def. Benchwarmers | 6–3 | Trevor Story (9 RBI, carried the Roughnecks offense) |
| Buttville Skidmarks vs. General Hospital | 4–4 TIE | Elly De La Cruz (4 SB, 2 HR, weekly engine) |
| Hiroshima Radiation def. Canberra Wombats | 8–2 | Bryce Harper (.552 OBP, Hiroshima's offensive anchor) |

---

## Thriller of the Week

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs 5, Albuquerque Bombers 4**

This one was the fantasy equivalent of a team winning a heavyweight boxing match on points when they got absolutely ragdolled for most of the fight. The **Wonderdogs** had no business winning this game offensively — 42 hits, a .306 OBP, and looking very much like a lineup assembled from the discount bin at a baseball card shop — but their pitching staff showed up like it was Game 7, dropping 62 strikeouts, a 1.13 WHIP, and 7 NSVH on the **Bombers** to steal enough categories to survive. (Seven NSVH! That's not pitching, that's bullying.) The **Bombers** — who now have the look of a team that will absolutely beat you in a scrimmage but can't close in real games — drop another one-score matchup and are officially starting to develop an identity nobody wants: the 2023 Tampa Bay Rays of BeerLeague Baseball. The **Wonderdogs** stay alive; the **Bombers** stay frustrated.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

**James Wood and Willson Contreras just turned Athletic Park into a Murderers' Row situation.**

Here's what happened: James Wood posted a .636 OBP. Willson Contreras posted a .594 OBP. Together they combined for 11 runs, 6 home runs, and 19 RBI. The **Athletic Park Dragons** scored 40 runs as a team and posted a .412 team OBP. And they did it against the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** — who, to their enormous credit, threw up a 1.65 ERA and 1.04 WHIP and still lost 6-3. (More on them in a moment, because that result is genuinely cursed.)

The Wood/Contreras thing needs to be addressed directly because what happened this week is not normal. A .636 OBP in a single week is the kind of number that makes you check the scoring settings three times before you believe it. The previous league record was .414 — set just two weeks ago — and Wood didn't just beat it, he lapped it. Contreras's .594 would have been the record *on its own* in any prior week, which means the **Dragons** essentially had the two greatest OBP weeks in league history happening simultaneously, on the same roster, at the same time. That's like if the '96 Bulls somehow had both the Jordan *and* Pippen seasons happening in the same week. It's not fair. The other 13 managers in this league should be upset.

Whether this is sustainable is a fair question. But the **Dragons** also have solid stolen base numbers (7 SB), six NSVH from their bullpen, and enough pitching to not be a complete liability. If Wood and Contreras keep this up at even 60% of this production level, **Athletic Park** is a legitimate title contender. You've been warned.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs def. Albuquerque Bombers, 5–4
The **Wonderdogs** won this game despite a .306 OBP and only 25 runs scored — because 62 Ks, a 1.13 WHIP, and 7 NSVH will do that for you. The **Bombers** pitched badly (5.05 ERA, 1.39 WHIP) and still nearly won, which tells you something about how thin the **Wonderdogs'** offense truly is. Strategically: the **Wonderdogs** are built like a 2000s-era ALCS pitcher-first team that needs to score 3 runs to win — it can work, but any pitching regression and this house of cards collapses. Next week the **Wonderdogs** need a viable bat off the wire, full stop.

### Sugar Land Skeeters def. Abbotsford-Colby Twins, 8–2
The **Skeeters** are doing that thing where great teams make it look boring. Nine stolen bases. Eight NSVH. A 2.11 ERA. They score from everywhere, they don't give up saves, and their pitching is currently functioning like a locked vault. The **Twins** had 75 strikeouts from their staff and zero NSVH, which is the fantasy equivalent of buying a sports car and forgetting to put gas in it. **Sugar Land** is the class of this league until further notice — everyone else is playing for second.

### Athletic Park Dragons def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 6–3
Look, I feel genuinely bad for **Wisconsin Rapids**. A 1.65 ERA. A 1.04 WHIP. Ten NSVH. And they *lost*. That's like acing the SATs and getting rejected from your safety school. The **Reapers** were simply on the wrong side of the Wood/Contreras supernova and there was nothing they could do about it. Once their offense (34 runs, .326 OBP — perfectly average) starts catching up to their elite pitching, this team becomes very dangerous. They're the sleeping giant of Week 3.

### BaseballPro77 def. North Correa, 5–3
The quietest 5-3 win in the league this week. **BaseballPro77** keeps doing exactly what they've done all season: mid-to-high marks across every category, solid pitching (3.59 ERA, 5 QS), and no glaring weaknesses. **North Correa** flashed the ceiling (Chris Sale's 22 Ks! Oneil Cruz's 11 combined SBs!) but a 4.73 ERA over 51 innings is the kind of floor that will haunt your playoff bracket. **BaseballPro77** is the most boring great team in the league and I mean that as a compliment.

### Riverside Roughnecks def. Benchwarmers, 6–3
Both pitching staffs were disasters (the **Roughnecks** posted zero — zero — quality starts with a 1.43 WHIP; the **Benchwarmers** threw 42.1 innings of 5.53 ERA ball), but Trevor Story's 9 RBI carried the **Roughnecks** to another 6-3 victory they probably shouldn't have. The **Benchwarmers** outscored them 30-23 in runs and somehow lost. More on that in a moment.

### Buttville Skidmarks vs. General Hospital, 4–4 TIE
Two teams walked away from this one feeling like they should have won. **Buttville** had a 2.25 ERA and Elly De La Cruz going full Elly (4 SB, 2 HR) and *still* couldn't get over the hump offensively. **General Hospital** had 70 strikeouts and 5 NSVH from their pitching staff and couldn't stop giving up runs (5.48 ERA, 1.45 WHIP). A tie is the fantasy baseball equivalent of a handshake deal nobody is happy about. Both teams leave frustrated.

### Hiroshima Radiation def. Canberra Wombats, 8–2
The **Radiation** is awake. After a shaky Week 2, they came back with 64 hits, 6 quality starts, 75 strikeouts, and Bryce Harper doing Bryce Harper things (.552 OBP). The **Wombats** hit 12 home runs — twelve! — and lost 8-2, which is what happens when your pitching gives up everything the power giveth. Gunnar Henderson is a real asset for **Canberra** but they need arms desperately before this season gets away from them.

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Luckiest: Benchwarmers (+0.91 luck delta)**

The **Benchwarmers** had the worst pitching week in the league — a 5.53 ERA, only 34 strikeouts in 42.1 innings, and zero dominant outings — and somehow posted a 6-3 win. That's the statistical equivalent of showing up to a job interview in pajamas and getting the job. The luck index is not fooled. The pitching will regress. The bill is coming.

**Unluckiest: Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs (-0.60 luck delta)**

The **Wonderdogs** threw 62 Ks, a 4.30 ERA, 1.13 WHIP, 5 quality starts, and 7 NSVH — a genuinely elite pitching performance — and their expected wins sat at 1.00. They got 0.4 actual wins. (Which is: half a win. In a 5-4 result. Against a team that threw a 5.05 ERA.) The offense is so bad it's actively canceling out what should be a dominant pitching narrative. This is the unluckiest team in the league and it's not particularly close.

---

## League Record: Shattered

Let's be formal about this for a second, because it deserves it.

**James Wood's .636 OBP is the new BeerLeague Baseball single-week record**, obliterating the previous mark of .414 set by **General Hospital** in Week 1. The old record stood for two whole weeks before Wood turned it into rubble. And Willson Contreras hit .594 OBP in the same week — which would have been the new record in any previous week in league history. Both records, same roster, same week. The **Athletic Park Dragons** did not come to play this week. They came to make history.

---

## Manager Spotlight: General Hospital

**General Hospital** is the most interesting 0-2-1 team in the league. (Yes, that record is what it is.) After setting the OBP league record in Week 1, they've been trending in the wrong direction on the standings — but the roster itself keeps flashing upside that makes you think the record is more bad luck than bad roster.

The Kumar Rocker add is a massive swing. You're essentially gambling that a top prospect's helium is worth rostering through the rough patches, and the 70 strikeouts in this week's tie shows the pitching staff has genuine swing-and-miss stuff. The problem is a 5.48 ERA that keeps betraying those strikeouts, and a luck delta of -0.54 suggests the team is genuinely underperforming what the underlying stats would predict.

Landing at #10 in this week's power rankings, **General Hospital** has a roster that — on paper — shouldn't be sitting at the bottom of the standings. If the ERA stabilizes and Rocker delivers, this could be the league's best "buy low" narrative by midseason. For now, they're the team everybody keeps meaning to take seriously right before they disappoint them.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**James Wood (Athletic Park Dragons):** Wood posted a .636 OBP in a single week and is clearly the most valuable fantasy asset in the league right now. **HOLD** — you don't sell this guy, you build around him. Any **Dragons** manager fielding calls on Wood should politely laugh and hang up.

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers' Pitching Staff:** A 1.65 ERA, 1.04 WHIP, and 10 NSVH in a *loss* is the most undervalued asset in the league. **BUY** — if you can pry any piece of this staff in a trade at a reasonable cost, do it. The offense will eventually click and this pitching will look like an absolute steal.

**Trevor Story (Riverside Roughnecks):** Nine RBI in one week is great. Zero quality starts from his supporting cast is not. **SELL** — Story is having a moment, but the **Roughnecks** roster structure makes him feel like a player you flip at peak value before the schedule catches up.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **Sugar Land Skeeters** ➡️ | Back-to-back elite weeks; 8 wins, 2.11 ERA, and an offense that scores from every position — nobody is touching this roster right now
2. **Athletic Park Dragons** ⬆️ | James Wood's .636 OBP and Contreras's .594 are not normal — 40 runs and .412 team OBP is a historic statement week; this team is ascending fast
3. **BaseballPro77** ➡️ | Jordan Walker's 5 HR explosion last week proved it wasn't a fluke; consistent top-5 category performer across all three weeks with solid pitching to match
4. **Hiroshima Radiation** ⬆️ | An 8–2 demolition of Canberra with 64 hits, league-leading 6 QS, and Bryce Harper's .552 OBP signals this team has fully woken up after a shaky Week 2
5. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** ⬇️ | A 1.65 ERA and 1.04 WHIP in a loss is the definition of unlucky — this pitching staff is elite and once the offense clicks, watch out
6. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** ⬆️ | Strongest pitching in the league over three weeks may finally be enough to climb; offense remains a structural crisis that one good waiver week won't fix
7. **North Correa** ⬇️ | Chris Sale's 22 Ks and Oneil Cruz's 6 SB show the ceiling is enormous, but 51 IP and a 4.73 ERA in a losing effort reveals the floor is still dangerous
8. **Albuquerque Bombers** ⬆️ | Three active adds including Josh Jung and Noelvi Marte show a manager trying to fix real problems; another 5–4 loss stings but the lineup is improving
9. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** ➡️ | Merrill Kelly is a smart add and the pitching was creditable with 75 Ks, but an 8–2 blowout loss and zero NSVH points to a roster that needs more help than one wire move
10. **General Hospital** ⬇️ | The spotlight team this week — Kumar Rocker is a big swing and the 70 Ks in a tie show pitching upside, but a 5.48 ERA and a luck delta of -0.54 means they're genuinely underperforming expectations
11. **Buttville Skidmarks** ➡️ | Elly De La Cruz is a weekly engine with
